summary: Fowler reflects on an unusual conference pairing where enterprise architecture and security were presented together, noting that attendees found the combination strange. He argues this reaction reveals a fundamental problem: security is wrongly treated as a separate, siloed concern rather than something that should pervade entire development teams. The post emphasizes that security cannot be bolted on through encapsulated classes but must be integrated into how teams think and work. Fowler endorses David LeBlanc's approach of prioritizing developer education over security review gates.
key_points: The fact that architecture and security are seen as 'diverse topics' reveals a problematic siloed mentality in the industry
Security cannot be added to applications through encapsulated classes - it requires team-wide thinking
Many eyeballs don't lead to secure code; you need many educated eyeballs
Developer education is more effective than relying on security review checkpoints
Internet-facing and corporate intranet applications especially require security thinking to pervade the team
money_quotes: Security is seen as some separate topic area which sits in its silo.
Security isn't something you can just add to an application by putting in a few encapsulated classes here and there.
Security thinking should pervade a whole team.
Many eyeballs don't lead to secure code - you need many educated eyeballs.
Educating developers is a key part of the picture, with less emphasis on review steps with security groups.
key_insight: Security must be treated as a cross-cutting concern that pervades team thinking, not a separate specialty that can be bolted on or delegated to review gates.
